text_store: # AccentOS Wiki — System Overview AccentOS is the internal operating system for Accent Lighting Inc., a commercial lighting distributor in Wichita, KS. It runs as a single-page app (vanilla JS, Cloudflare Pages, Supabase backend) serving 5 roles: Owner, Admin, Manager, Sales, Warehouse. ## Core modules (live) | Module | Key | Description | |--------|-----|-------------| | Dashboard | dashboard | Daily Brief + role-specific KPIs | | Sales Pipeline | pipeline | Kanban deals + probability model | | Customers | customers | CRM, RFM, lifecycle tracking | | Quote Generator | quotes | Line-item quoting with AI summary | | Job Tracker | jobs | Project work tracking | | Purchase Orders | purchaseorders | Vendor POs + receipt flow | | Trade Partners | tradepartners | Designers, contractors, architects | | Warranty Tracker | warranty | Defective claims + vendor RMAs | | Showroom Displays | showrooms | Display program tracking | | Labels | labels | QR + barcode printing | | Deliveries | deliveries | Schedule + routing | | Decision Engine | decisionengine | Pure-compute recommendations | | Competitive Pricing | competitive | Competitor price tracking | | Demand Forecast | demandforecast | Velocity +

# Rubric: Rebates (Weight: 10) Highest-weight financial category. Rebates are year-end backend payments from vendor to dealer based on total purchase volume. Directly improves net margin without affecting list pricing. ## 0–10 Scale | Score | Threshold | |-------|-----------| | 10 | ≥6% rebate | | 9 | 5–5.9% | | 8 | 4–4.9% | | 7 | 3–3.9% | | 6 | 2.5–2.9% | | 5 | 2–2.4% | | 4 | 1.5–1.9% | | 3 | 1–1.4% | | 2 | <1% | | 1 | Exists but effectively useless (too complex/unattainable) | | 0 | No rebate program | ## Notes - Industry average for Accent's vendor tier: ~2–3% (score 5–7). - Tiered rebates common: first $X purchases at 1%, next tranche at 2%, etc. Score the blended expected rate. - Some vendors offer rebates only to Lighting One members — factor in if Accent is/isn't qualified. - Rebates payable January–March for prior calendar year. Cash flow timing matters for score adjustment. ## Related [[vendor-scoring]] · [[rubric-discounts]] · [[rubric-credit-terms]]
timing matters for score adjustment. ## Related [[vendor-scoring]] · [[rubric-discounts]] · [[rubric-credit-terms]]

# Rubric: DTC (Weight: 7) How aggressively the vendor competes with its own dealers through direct-to-consumer sales. Lower DTC aggression = better for Accent's margins and competitive position. ## 0–10 Scale | Score | DTC behavior | |-------|-------------| | 10 | No DTC — refers consumers specifically to Accent | | 9 | No DTC — routes to lighting showrooms generally | | 8 | Brand site exists, no checkout, dealer-only purchase path | | 7 | — | | 6 | — | | 5 | Sells via showrooms + ecom dealers (Lumens/YLighting), no own ecom (average) | | 4 | Showrooms + ecom dealers + light DTC | | 3 | Sells through designers/trade only (exclusive channel) | | 2 | Active DTC + Amazon — competing directly with dealers | | 1 | Aggressive DTC — outranks dealers in Google search | | 0 | Sells DTC on own website, no dealer protection | ## Notes - Scores 7 and 6 are unused — the gap between "dealer locator only" and "ecom dealers" is real. - Score 3 (designers/trade only) is unusual: premium brands like Visual Comfort trade-only means no consumer price competition but also limits Accent's

Scores 7 and 6 are unused — the gap between "dealer locator only" and "ecom dealers" is real. - Score 3 (designers/trade only) is unusual: premium brands like Visual Comfort trade-only means no consumer price competition but also limits Accent's consumer sales. - Amazon presence is a major negative signal — it commoditizes the product and kills showroom justification. - Strong IMAP ([[rubric-imap]]) combined with DTC score 5+ = well-managed channel. ## Related [[vendor-scoring]] · [[rubric-imap]] · [[rubric-web-listing]]

# Emergency Lighting Compliance ## Key codes | Code | Scope | Requirement | |------|-------|-------------| | NEC Article 700 | Emergency systems | Automatic transfer in ≤10 seconds | | NEC Article 701 | Legally required standby | Within 60 seconds | | NEC Article 702 | Optional standby | As specified | | NFPA 101 (Life Safety Code) | Egress illumination | 1 FC at floor, path of egress | | IBC 1008 | Exit/egress lighting | ≥1 FC average, ≤0.1 FC minimum | | IECC / ASHRAE 90.1 | Commercial energy | Emergency circuits exempt from power density | ## Egress illumination requirements (NFPA 101 / IBC) - **Minimum at floor**: 1.0 FC (10.76 lux) along means of egress - **Duration**: 90 minutes minimum on battery backup - **Coverage**: every exit discharge, exit access corridor, stairway, ramp - **Failure**: single lamp failure cannot reduce illumination below 0.2 FC ## Exit signs - Letter height ≥ 6 inches - Stroke width: ≥ 3/4 inch - Visibility: legible from 100 feet (IBC) - NEC 700.16: must be connected to emergency circuit - Combined exit/emergency combos common in commercial — saves circuit wiring ## Battery backup types | Type | Pros

Stroke width: ≥ 3/4 inch - Visibility: legible from 100 feet (IBC) - NEC 700.16: must be connected to emergency circuit - Combined exit/emergency combos common in commercial — saves circuit wiring ## Battery backup types | Type | Pros | Cons | Applications | |------|------|------|-------------| | Self-contained (integral) | Simple, no central battery | Periodic testing required | Small commercial | | Central battery system | Centralized monitoring | Higher install cost | Large buildings | | Generator transfer | Extended backup | 10-sec transfer delay | Critical facilities | | LED emergency driver | Drop-in for LED fixtures | 90-min battery limit | Retrofit projects | ## Common products at Accent - Emergency/exit combos (bug-eye + exit sign) - LED emergency drivers (retrofit kits for existing LED fixtures) - Self-contained emergency LED fixtures - Remote-capable exit signs ## Testing requirements - Monthly: 30-second functional test - Annual: 90-minute full-duration test - Documentation: test log required by AHJ ## Related [[lighting-reference]] · [[lumen-output-commercial]] · [[dimming-protocols]]

# SOP: Rep Outreach Standard process for initiating and maintaining manufacturer rep relationships. ## Outreach triggers - New vendor onboarding (see [[sop-vendor-onboarding]]) - Rep score ([[rubric-rep-score]]) drops below 5 → escalate - No rep contact in >90 days → re-engage - Co-op/MDF claim pending → pursue resolution - New promo or product line launch → request presentation - Competitive bid requiring vendor support → request pricing/spec help ## Initial outreach (new vendor or new rep) 1. Identify rep from vendor website or L1 rep locator 2. Email introduction: - Introduce Accent Lighting (location, focus, volume segment) - Request an intro meeting (in-person if nearby, Zoom/phone if remote) - Ask for price list access + wholesale account setup 3. Prep for intro meeting: - Review vendor's top-selling SKUs and price points - Know current competing products Accent carries - Come with specific questions (see intake list below) ## Intro meeting question list - Freight: free threshold? Drop-ship available? - Terms: net days? Credit limit process? - Rebates: what % at our volume? Tiered? - MDF/co-op: % available? How do we claim? - Display program: discount? Buyback policy? Current promos? - IMAP: enforced? Monitoring process? - Returns: restock fee? RGA process and

days? Credit limit process? - Rebates: what % at our volume? Tiered? - MDF/co-op: % available? How do we claim? - Display program: discount? Buyback policy? Current promos? - IMAP: enforced? Monitoring process? - Returns: restock fee? RGA process and timeline? - Support: who's our dedicated rep? Territory coverage? - L1: are you a Lighting One vendor? Lights America listing? - Digital assets: portal access? Image/spec sheet quality? ## Ongoing rep management - Log every interaction in AccentOS Rep Outreach module - Update rep score quarterly (or after significant interaction) - Flag if rep changes → re-score and re-engage - Send quarterly performance recap: Accent's purchases, opens, pending ## Rep score escalation If rep score drops below 4: 1. Document specific failures (missed callbacks, wrong pricing, etc.) 2. Contact vendor's regional manager directly 3. Request rep reassignment if pattern persists 4. Note escalation in AccentOS vendor notes ## Related [[vendor-scoring]] · [[rubric-rep-score]] · [[sop-vendor-onboarding]] · [[michael-graf]]

# SOP: Quote Creation Standard process for building and delivering a commercial lighting quote. ## Pre-quote: scope intake Gather from customer/specifier: - Project type (office, retail, warehouse, restaurant, residential, etc.) - Square footage by space type - Existing infrastructure: ceiling height, mounting type, conduit, dimming protocol - Budget range ($/SF or total) - Timeline: bid due date, project start, substantial completion - Decision-maker role (owner, GC, electrician, designer) - Spec set or open spec? (if open: you can spec Accent products; if specified: substitution process) ## Product selection 1. Match space type → lumen target (see [[lumen-output-commercial]]) 2. Confirm CCT requirement ([[color-temperature-selection]]) 3. Check CRI spec — ≥80 standard commercial, ≥90 for retail/medical ([[cri-tm30-tlci]]) 4. Check emergency lighting code requirements ([[emergency-lighting-compliance]]) 5. Confirm dimming protocol ([[dimming-protocols]]) 6. Select products from Accent's vendor lineup; prioritize high-score vendors ([[vendor-scoring]]) ## Building the quote in AccentOS 1. Open Quote Generator → New Quote 2. Set customer, project name, type, square footage, budget 3. Add line items: product, qty, unit net cost, unit list 4. AI summary auto-generates via Claude API 5. Review for: freight applicability, lead time flags, missing items (emergency, controls) 6. Adjust margin if needed: standard commercial = 40–45% GP ##

Add line items: product, qty, unit net cost, unit list 4. AI summary auto-generates via Claude API 5. Review for: freight applicability, lead time flags, missing items (emergency, controls) 6. Adjust margin if needed: standard commercial = 40–45% GP ## Pricing rules - Standard GP target: 40–45% on product; 35% minimum - Freight: check vendor free-freight threshold — include in quote if below threshold - Lead times: flag long-lead items (>4 weeks) explicitly in quote - List price: never show net cost to customer; show list price with discount % OR show net with "your price" - IMAP: never quote below vendor IMAP on branded products ## Delivery - Email quote PDF + product cut sheets - Follow up in 48–72 hours if no response - Competitive situations: authorized substitution requires matching spec sheet attributes (lumen output, CCT, CRI, dimming, listing) ## Quote → Job conversion If customer awards project: use Deal → Job preset in AccentOS Pipeline (goTo pipeline → openDeal → Create Job button). ## Related [[sop-vendor-onboarding]] · [[lumen-output-commercial]] · [[color-temperature-selection]] · [[cri-tm30-tlci]]

# Rubric: Web Listing (Weight: 4) How prominently Accent Lighting appears on the vendor's dealer locator or "where to buy" page. Higher = more inbound consumer traffic routed to Accent. ## 0–10 Scale | Score | Listing quality | |-------|----------------| | 10 | Featured/premier dealer (logo + top placement) | | 9 | Prominent with map pin, link, full info | | 8 | Listed with link + accurate contact info | | 7 | Standard dealer locator listing | | 6 | — | | 5 | Listed but stale or incomplete info | | 4 | — | | 3 | No locator — directs to "contact your rep" | | 2 | — | | 1 | Authorized but not on locator | | 0 | Not on vendor site at all | ## Notes - Lowest weight of all categories (4) — it matters but is easily fixed with a rep call. - Premier dealer status may require minimum annual purchase volume. - Stale listings (wrong phone, old address, closed hours) actively hurt — verify annually. - Some vendors only maintain locators for "preferred" dealers; authorization alone doesn't guarantee listing. ## Related [[vendor-scoring]] · [[rubric-dtc]] ·

# Rubric: IMAP / Markup (Weight: 8) Internet Minimum Advertised Price (IMAP) protection and achievable retail markup multiplier. Higher IMAP = protected margin, reduced price-matching pressure from online competitors. ## 0–10 Scale | Score | Markup multiplier | |-------|------------------| | 10 | 2.5× or higher (keystone-plus) | | 9 | 2.3–2.49× | | 8 | 2.1–2.29× | | 7 | 2.0–2.09× (solid keystone) | | 6 | 1.9–1.99× | | 5 | 1.8–1.89× (average) | | 4 | 1.6–1.79× | | 3 | 1.4–1.59× | | 2 | 1.2–1.39× | | 1 | <1.2× markup | | 0 | No IMAP / open pricing (race to bottom) | ## Notes - Markup multiplier = MSRP ÷ net cost. A $100 net fixture with $200 MSRP = 2.0×. - Vendors without IMAP enforcement create channel conflict — Amazon/online undercutters kill showroom margins. - Strong DTC score ([[rubric-dtc]]) and strong IMAP often co-occur: vendors that protect dealers protect pricing. - Lighting fixtures typically run 1.8–2.2× at showroom. 2.5×+ is premium territory (Hudson Valley, Visual Comfort). ## Related [[vendor-scoring]] · [[rubric-dtc]] · [[rubric-rebates]]

# Rubric: Display Program (Weight: 6) Showroom display support: discount on display product, promotional cadence, and buyback/refresh program. ## Composite scoring (RUBRIC_COMPONENTS) Points breakdown (max 10): | Component | Points | |-----------|--------| | Display discount ≥60% | 6 pts | | Display discount ≥50% | 5 pts | | Display discount ≥35% | 3 pts | | Display discount ≥25% | 2 pts | | Display discount ≥10% | 1 pt | | Promo cadence: monthly+ | 2 pts | | Promo cadence: quarterly | 2 pts | | Promo cadence: occasional | 1 pt | | Buyback: generous (funded refresh) | 2 pts | | Buyback: standard (annual swap) | 2 pts | | Buyback: limited (case by case) | 1 pt | ## 0–10 Rubric guide | Score | Combination | |-------|-------------| | 10 | 60%+ disc + monthly promo + generous buyback | | 9 | 60%+ disc + quarterly promo + standard buyback | | 8 | 50%+ disc + monthly promo + standard buyback | | 7 | 35%+ disc + quarterly promo + standard buyback | | 6 | 25–35% disc + occasional promo + standard buyback | | 5 | 25% disc +

| 50%+ disc + monthly promo + standard buyback | | 7 | 35%+ disc + quarterly promo + standard buyback | | 6 | 25–35% disc + occasional promo + standard buyback | | 5 | 25% disc + occasional promo + limited buyback (average) | | 4 | 25% disc + no promo + limited buyback | | 3 | 10–25% disc + occasional promo + no buyback | | 2 | Any disc + no promo + no buyback | | 1 | <25% disc + no promo + no buyback | | 0 | No display program | ## Notes - Buyback/refresh programs are critical for floor model turnover. Stale displays hurt sales. - "Generous" buyback = vendor funds replacement fixtures or offers 1-for-1 swap annually. - Promo cadence drives foot traffic + promotional email opportunities. - High display discount (50%+) makes it economical to display lower-velocity product. ## Related [[vendor-scoring]] · [[rubric-marketing-funds]] · [[rubric-returns]]

# Rubric: Rep Score (Weight: 8) Quality of the manufacturer's rep assigned to Accent. Covers responsiveness, proactivity, problem-solving, and relationship depth. ## 0–10 Scale | Score | Rep quality | |-------|------------| | 10 | Proactive, full support — anticipates needs, solves before asked | | 9 | Excellent — fast issue resolution, strong partnership | | 8 | Very good — reliable, follows through, occasional delays | | 7 | Good — reliable but rarely proactive | | 6 | Adequate — handles requests, no above-and-beyond | | 5 | Average — standard performance | | 4 | Below average — slow, inconsistent | | 3 | Reactive only — never initiates contact | | 2 | Rarely helpful — hard to reach, poor follow-through | | 1 | Unreliable — misses commitments, damages relationship | | 0 | No rep assigned — Accent services account directly | ## Notes - Rep quality is the most subjective category. # Lumen Output — Commercial Applications ## Footcandle targets by space type | Space | FC (footcandles) | Notes | |-------|-----------------|-------| | Open office | 30–50 FC | IECC: ≤0.6 W/SF | | Private office | 30–50 FC | Task supplement common | | Conference room | 30–50 FC | Dimming for presentations | | Retail general | 30–50 FC | Boost merchandise to 100+ FC | | Retail feature display | 100–150 FC | Accent (track/spots) | | Restaurant dining | 10–30 FC | Ambiance-driven; dim low | | Restaurant kitchen | 50–70 FC | NSF: 50 FC min at work surfaces | | Warehouse aisle | 20–30 FC | AGV/forklift zones higher | | Warehouse picking | 50–75 FC | Task-critical accuracy | | Parking garage | 3–5 FC maintained | IES RP-20-14 | | Corridor/hallway | 10–20 FC | Emergency: 1 FC min path | | Hotel lobby | 20–50 FC | Varies by brand standard | ## Lumen-per-watt efficiency - Fluorescent T8 (32W 4-lamp): ~80–100 LPW - LED troffer (40W equivalent): ~100–130 LPW - LED high bay: 130–180 LPW (premium: 180–200+) - Metal halide (400W): ~65–80 LPW (obsolete) - LED retrofit: 130–160 LPW ## Calculating fixture

## Lumen-per-watt efficiency - Fluorescent T8 (32W 4-lamp): ~80–100 LPW - LED troffer (40W equivalent): ~100–130 LPW - LED high bay: 130–180 LPW (premium: 180–200+) - Metal halide (400W): ~65–80 LPW (obsolete) - LED retrofit: 130–160 LPW ## Calculating fixture count ``` Fixtures = (Target FC × Room SF) / (Fixture lumens × CU × LLF) ``` - **CU** (Coefficient of Utilization): 0.5–0.8 typical; from photometric data - **LLF** (Light Loss Factor): 0.8 typical (accounts for dirt, lamp depreciation) - **FC to lux**: 1 FC = 10.764 lux ## Common product categories at Accent - Linear LED (troffers, strips): office, retail backroom, warehouse - LED high bay: warehouse, distribution, gym - Track/accent: retail merchandise display, showroom - Downlights (4", 6"): office, hospitality, residential - Emergency/exit combo: required in all commercial egress paths ## Related [[lighting-reference]] · [[color-temperature-selection]] · [[emergency-lighting-compliance]]

# SOP: Vendor Onboarding Standard process for adding a new vendor to AccentOS and establishing the business relationship. ## Steps ### 1. Initial qualification (Michael + Sales) - Confirm vendor is not a DTC-first brand (check [[rubric-dtc]]) - Verify no direct Amazon shop competing with dealers - Check L1 membership status ([[rubric-l1-member]]) - Confirm they sell to independent lighting showrooms (not big-box exclusive) ### 2. Rep contact and intro meeting - Follow [[sop-rep-outreach]] to establish rep contact - Request: wholesale price list, net terms offer, freight policy, rebate program - Ask: IMAP enforcement policy, return/RGA process, co-op/MDF % available ### 3. Score in AccentOS - Open Vendor Ranking → Add vendor - Enter all 14 rubric scores based on rep conversation - Mark categories as `unverified` until confirmed in writing - Set data state = `needs_review` until first PO ships ### 4. Account setup - Request credit application form - Submit Accent's resale certificate / tax exemption - Agree on terms in writing (net days, freight threshold, rebate tiers) - Get login credentials for vendor portal (price lists, digital assets) ### 5. First order - Place initial stocking order (qualify for freight threshold) - Verify pricing against price list before

terms in writing (net days, freight threshold, rebate tiers) - Get login credentials for vendor portal (price lists, digital assets) ### 5. First order - Place initial stocking order (qualify for freight threshold) - Verify pricing against price list before PO submission - Log PO in AccentOS → Purchase Orders ### 6. Showroom display - If vendor has display program ([[rubric-display]]): request display pricing, current promo - Plan floor placement — coordinate with Paul (showroom manager) - Document display fixtures in AccentOS Showroom Displays module ### 7. Post-90-day review - Update vendor score from `unverified` to `scored` based on live experience - Adjust rep score ([[rubric-rep-score]]) from early interactions - Verify all terms match what was promised in step 2 ## Owner/contact Michael Graf (Owner) approves all new vendor adds. Paul Graf handles floor placement. ## Related [[vendor-scoring]] · [[sop-rep-outreach]] · [[michael-graf]] · [[paul-graf]]

# Rubric: Lights America (Weight: 5) Binary: is the vendor listed on LightsAmerica.com? This is the Lighting One buying group's consumer-facing discovery site. Listing = vendor partnership with L1, increased consumer exposure for Accent's brands. ## Scale | Score | Condition | |-------|-----------| | 10 | Listed on Lights America | | 0 | Not on Lights America | ## Notes - LightsAmerica.com is the Lighting One member-brand consumer portal. Vendors listed there are L1-preferred or L1-approved partners. - Scores 1–9 are unused — this is a binary gate, not a spectrum. - Often correlated with [[rubric-l1-member]]: vendors on Lights America tend to also be L1 members. - Key for Accent's buying group leverage: L1 membership + Lights America listing = full stack of L1 purchasing benefits. ## Related [[vendor-scoring]] · [[rubric-l1-member]] · [[rubric-web-listing]]

# Rubric: Freight (Weight: 8) Free-freight threshold: the minimum order dollar amount to qualify for free shipping. Lower threshold = cheaper to stock lower-velocity items. Higher threshold forces batch ordering. ## 0–10 Scale | Score | Free freight threshold | |-------|----------------------| | 10 | Free on any order (<$500 threshold) | | 9 | Free at $500–$749 | | 8 | Free at $750–$999 | | 7 | Free at $1,000–$1,499 | | 6 | Free at $1,500–$1,999 | | 5 | Free at $2,000–$2,499 (average) | | 4 | Free at $2,500–$2,999 | | 3 | Free at $3,000–$3,499 | | 2 | Free at $3,500–$3,999 | | 1 | Free at $4,000+ | | 0 | No free freight — always prepaid or third-party | ## Notes - RUBRIC_NUMERIC in AccentOS uses `dir: 'lower_better'` for the threshold value. - Drop-ship programs may have separate freight rules — score the primary stock order path. - Some vendors offer free freight on select product lines or promotional periods — score the best available for Accent's typical order size. - Freight adds ~3–8% landed cost when not free; critical for margin on low-margin product. ## Related [[vendor-scoring]] · [[rubric-returns]] · [[rubric-discounts]]

# Karpathy LLM Wiki Pattern Andrej Karpathy's proposal: instead of training models on fresh data continuously, maintain a curated "LLM Wiki" — a set of human-readable, dense, cross-linked markdown documents that serve as the grounding layer for an LLM. The LLM retrieves relevant pages and uses them as context rather than relying on parametric memory. ## Core principles 1. **Human-readable first**: pages are useful to humans, not just machines. No embedding-only content. 2. **Density over coverage**: 500 words of signal beats 5000 words of fluff. Edit ruthlessly. 3. **Cross-links as knowledge graph**: double-bracket slug wikilinks between pages build a traversable graph the LLM can follow. 4. **Confidence-tagged**: every page has a confidence score so the LLM knows when to hedge. 5. **Zero extra infra**: pure markdown files, fetched over HTTP. No vector DB required for the primary path. ## Why it works for AccentOS AccentOS knowledge is: - Stable (vendor scores don't change daily) - Domain-specific (no public LLM training data covers Accent's vendor relationships) - Cross-linked (rubric categories reference each other, SOPs reference entities) - Human-curated (Michael's judgment is the signal; automated extraction is secondary) ## Three-layer architecture ``` Layer 1: Raw sources (MASTER.md, js/, sql/, index.html) ↓ summarize

data covers Accent's vendor relationships) - Cross-linked (rubric categories reference each other, SOPs reference entities) - Human-curated (Michael's judgment is the signal; automated extraction is secondary) ## Three-layer architecture ``` Layer 1: Raw sources (MASTER.md, js/, sql/, index.html) ↓ summarize Layer 2: wiki/ markdown pages ← LLM Wiki (primary grounding) ↓ index Layer 3: BM25 + dense index ← optional dev tool ``` ## How Ask the Engine uses it 1. User sends a message 2. Extract key terms 3. Scan wiki/index.md for slug matches 4. Fetch top-3 pages 5. Inject as grounding context into system prompt 6. Render "Grounded · N wiki sources" pill with click-through links ## Comparison to pure RAG | Dimension | Wiki pattern | pgvector RAG | |-----------|-------------|-------------| | Infra cost | $0 | Supabase M42/M43 | | Latency | ~50ms (fetch) | ~200ms (embed + query) | | Freshness | Manual update | Auto-ingest | | Accuracy | High (human-curated) | Variable (chunk quality) | | Explainability | Full (you can read the page) | Opaque chunks | | Fallback | Always works | Requires live DB | ## Related [[ADR-007]]

# Rubric: Demand / Brand Awareness (Weight: 10) Consumer brand search volume (monthly US searches). # Color Temperature Selection ## CCT scale reference | CCT | Name | Appearance | Applications | |-----|------|-----------|-------------| | 2200K | Ultra-warm | Candlelight | Upscale restaurant, hospitality | | 2700K | Warm white | Incandescent-like | Residential, restaurant, hotel | | 3000K | Warm white | Slightly whiter | Retail, residential, hospitality | | 3500K | Neutral white | Clean white | Office, retail, healthcare | | 4000K | Cool white | Bright neutral | Office, educational, commercial | | 5000K | Daylight | Blue-white | Warehouse, industrial, garage | | 5700–6500K | Bright daylight | Cool blue | Task, security, outdoor | ## Selection by application **Residential showroom recommendations** - Living room: 2700K or 3000K - Kitchen ambient: 3000K; task: 3000–3500K - Master bath: 3000K (critical: cosmetic accuracy) - Bedroom: 2700–3000K - Outdoor: 3000K (warm) or 4000K (security) **Commercial** - Retail apparel: 3000K (warm, flattering) - Retail food: 3000K (warm enhances appetite) - Retail jewelry: 4000–4500K (cool, high sparkle) - Office open plan: 3500–4000K - Healthcare: 3500–4000K (exam rooms may need 4000–5000K) - Warehouse/industrial: 5000K (alertness, high contrast) ## CCT consistency rule All fixtures in a space should be within 100K of each other. Mixing 3000K and 4000K

sparkle) - Office open plan: 3500–4000K - Healthcare: 3500–4000K (exam rooms may need 4000–5000K) - Warehouse/industrial: 5000K (alertness, high contrast) ## CCT consistency rule All fixtures in a space should be within 100K of each other. Mixing 3000K and 4000K in the same room creates visible discontinuity. When a customer asks about mixing, recommend single-CCT throughout with accent layers at same CCT. ## Tunable white (TW) Tunable white fixtures shift CCT from ~2700K to 6500K via dual LED channels. Used in: - Healthcare (circadian support) - Education (alertness tuning across school day) - Hospitality (ambiance shifts from dining to morning briefing) - Adds 20–40% cost premium over fixed-CCT ## Kelvin vs CRI (distinct metrics) CCT is *color appearance* (warm/cool axis). CRI is *color rendering accuracy*. A 3000K, CRI 70 fixture looks warm but renders colors poorly. A 3000K, CRI 95 fixture looks warm AND renders colors accurately. See [[cri-tm30-tlci]] for quality metrics. ## Related [[lighting-reference]] · [[cri-tm30-tlci]] · [[lumen-output-commercial]]

# Vendor Scoring System Accent Lighting scores every vendor on a 14-category, 0–10 rubric. The weighted sum produces a single score used to rank vendors, gate co-op spend, prioritize showroom space, and route sales reps. ## Category weights (live in AccentOS) **Financial Terms** (weights in parentheses): | Category | Weight | Key rubric | |----------|--------|-----------| | Rebates | 10 | ≥6% = 10; 0% = 0 | | Discounts | 8 | ≥20% = 10; 0% = 0 | | Credit Terms | 6 | Net 120+ = 10; No credit = 0 | | Freight | 8 | Free <$500 = 10; No free freight = 0 | | Returns/RGA | 7 | Free returns <7d = 10; None = 0 | | IMAP/Markup | 8 | 2.5× = 10; No IMAP = 0 | **Sales, Marketing & Market Position**: | Category | Weight | Key rubric | |----------|--------|-----------| | Marketing Funds | 7 | MDF + assets + cobrand = up to 10 | | Display Program | 6 | Discount + promos + buyback = up to 10 | | Lights America | 5 | Binary: on = 10; off = 0 | | Web Listing |

cobrand = up to 10 | | Display Program | 6 | Discount + promos + buyback = up to 10 | | Lights America | 5 | Binary: on = 10; off = 0 | | Web Listing | 4 | Featured dealer = 10; not on site = 0 | | Rep Score | 8 | Proactive full support = 10; no rep = 0 | | DTC | 7 | No DTC + refers to Accent = 10; sells DTC = 0 | | L1 Member | 6 | Lighting One member = 10; not = 0 | | Demand | 10 | 100K+ monthly searches = 10; 0 = 0 | Total weight: 100 (weights are percentages in the weighted sum). ## Scoring formula `weightedScore = Σ(score_i × weight_i) / Σ(weight_i)` Each category score is 0–10. The weighted average is the vendor's final score. ## Tier system (adaptive) Tiers are computed from the live score distribution: - **S-tier**: top 10% of scored vendors - **A-tier**: 10–30% - **B-tier**: 30–60% - **C-tier**: 60–80% - **D-tier**: bottom 20% Tier boundaries recalculate as new scores are added. ## Score states Each category can be: `scored | unverified | needs_review

- **S-tier**: top 10% of scored vendors - **A-tier**: 10–30% - **B-tier**: 30–60% - **C-tier**: 60–80% - **D-tier**: bottom 20% Tier boundaries recalculate as new scores are added. ## Score states Each category can be: `scored | unverified | needs_review | n/a` Data state tracked in `vendor_score_states` Supabase table. ## Usage in AccentOS - Vendor Ranking page: primary sort + tier grouping - Daily Brief: surfaces low-scoring key vendors - Deal Optimizer: flags vendors below score threshold - Co-op tracker: gates spend recommendations - Rep Outreach: prioritizes outreach queue ## Related rubric pages [[rubric-rebates]] · [[rubric-discounts]] · [[rubric-credit-terms]] · [[rubric-freight]] · [[rubric-returns]] · [[rubric-imap]] · [[rubric-marketing-funds]] · [[rubric-display]] · [[rubric-lights-america]] · [[rubric-web-listing]] · [[rubric-rep-score]] · [[rubric-dtc]] · [[rubric-l1-member]] · [[rubric-demand]]

# CRI, TM-30, and TLCI Standards ## CRI (Color Rendering Index) — Ra Legacy metric. 0–100 scale; 100 = perfect color rendering under reference illuminant. | CRI Range | Quality | Applications | |-----------|---------|-------------| | 90–100 | Excellent | Retail, museum, medical, residential premium | | 80–89 | Good | Office, commercial, most retail | | 70–79 | Acceptable | Industrial, warehouse, utility | | <70 | Poor | Storage, parking, outdoor areas only | **Limits of CRI**: Ra averages 8 color samples, all pastel. Misses saturated colors (R9 = red saturation). A fixture can have CRI 90 but render reds poorly. Always ask for R9 separately. **R9 threshold**: R9 ≥ 50 = good red rendering. R9 ≥ 80 = premium. Critical for: retail, food, medical, cosmetic applications. ## TM-30-18/20 (IES Technical Memorandum) Two metrics: **Rf** (Fidelity) and **Rg** (Gamut). | Metric | Range | Meaning | |--------|-------|---------| | Rf | 0–100 | Average color fidelity (like CRI Ra, but 99 samples) | | Rg | 60–140 | Color saturation. 100 = neutral; >100 = boosted saturation; <100 = muted | **Rf ≥ 78** = code minimum for most commercial. **Rf ≥ 85, Rg 95–110** = retail sweet spot (accurate

99 samples) | | Rg | 60–140 | Color saturation. 100 = neutral; >100 = boosted saturation; <100 = muted | **Rf ≥ 78** = code minimum for most commercial. **Rf ≥ 85, Rg 95–110** = retail sweet spot (accurate + slightly vivid). **Rf ≥ 90** = premium / museum / medical. Rg > 100 makes colors pop (common in retail/food — "looks more vibrant"). But at Rg 120+ colors become unnatural. ## TLCI (Television Lighting Consistency Index) Metric for broadcast/film. 0–100. - TLCI ≥ 85: broadcast-acceptable - TLCI ≥ 95: studio-grade Relevant for Accent only when specifying showroom or studio lighting for content-creator customers. ## Practical spec checklist When quoting commercial jobs, request from vendor: 1. CRI Ra (expect ≥80; specify ≥90 for retail) 2. R9 value (specify ≥50 for any food/apparel/cosmetic application) 3. TM-30 Rf and Rg (if customer is design-focused or upscale retail) 4. Correlated Color Temperature (CCT) tolerance: ±50K or ±100K? ## Related [[lighting-reference]] · [[color-temperature-selection]] · [[lumen-output-commercial]]

# Dimming Protocols ## Protocol comparison | Protocol | Signal | Range | Applications | Cost | |----------|--------|-------|-------------|------| | Triac (leading edge) | AC phase cut | 0–100% | Residential, small commercial | Low | | ELV (trailing edge) | AC phase cut | 0–100% | LED drivers, electronic LV | Low | | 0-10V | Analog DC voltage | 10%–100% | Commercial LED, HID retrofit | Medium | | DALI (IEC 62386) | Digital addressable | 0.1%–100% | Commercial, hospitality, offices | High | | Casambi / Zigbee / Bluetooth | Wireless mesh | 0–100% | Retrofit, residential smart | Medium | | DMX-512 | Digital multiplex | 0–100% | Entertainment, theatrical | High | | Phase selectable | Auto-detect | 0–100% | Universal drivers | Medium | ## 0-10V (most common commercial) - Two-wire analog (0V = off, 10V = full); LED driver interprets voltage - Simple, inexpensive, widely compatible - **Limitation**: dims to ~10% minimum before "step-off" (fixture cuts off); no true 0% without switching relay - Common with occupancy sensors, daylight sensors in IECC-compliant offices ## DALI (Digital Addressable Lighting Interface) - Each fixture gets unique address; individual control from 2-wire bus - True 0.1% dimming

(fixture cuts off); no true 0% without switching relay - Common with occupancy sensors, daylight sensors in IECC-compliant offices ## DALI (Digital Addressable Lighting Interface) - Each fixture gets unique address; individual control from 2-wire bus - True 0.1% dimming (smooth fade to black) - Bidirectional: driver reports back lamp failure, burn hours - DALI-2: standardized, multi-manufacturer interoperability - **Use case**: lobbies, conference centers, hospitality, any scene-based control - Higher wiring and commissioning cost than 0-10V ## Triac / ELV (residential) - Triac (forward phase cut): older, suited for resistive/incandescent loads; causes flicker with LED if mismatched - ELV (reverse phase cut): better for capacitive/electronic loads; preferred for LED retrofit - Must match dimmer type to LED driver — mismatch = flicker, buzz, incompatibility - Always provide dimmer compatibility list from vendor ## Flicker and TLA - **TLA (Temporal Light Artefact)**: includes flicker, stroboscopic effect, phantom array - IEEE 1789-2015: thresholds for flicker that causes health/visual discomfort - Percent flicker: `(max - min) / (max + min) × 100`. Target: <15% at dimmed levels - Critical for task lighting, video production, healthcare ## Practical guidance for quotes - Office/retail: default to 0-10V (simple, cost-effective, code-compliant) - Hospitality/high-end commercial: spec DALI

`(max - min) / (max + min) × 100`. Target: <15% at dimmed levels - Critical for task lighting, video production, healthcare ## Practical guidance for quotes - Office/retail: default to 0-10V (simple, cost-effective, code-compliant) - Hospitality/high-end commercial: spec DALI for scene control flexibility - Residential/small commercial retrofit: ELV dimmer + compatible LED driver - Always ask: "Is there existing dimming infrastructure? What protocol?" - Dimmer compatibility list required with every LED quote involving dimming ## Related [[lighting-reference]] · [[color-temperature-selection]] · [[emergency-lighting-compliance]]

# Source: Karpathy LLM Wiki Gist **Origin**: Andrej Karpathy's public writing on LLM knowledge grounding **Layer**: External reference (no file in repo; concepts extracted to wiki) ## Core ideas (extracted) 1. Maintain a curated, human-readable wiki of domain knowledge 2. LLM retrieves relevant pages as context (not parametric memory) 3. Dense, cross-linked markdown → traversable knowledge graph 4. Confidence tagging lets LLM calibrate hedging 5. Human editing is the quality gate; auto-ingest is secondary 6. Zero infra: pure markdown files, HTTP fetch, no vector DB required for primary path ## Adaptation for AccentOS AccentOS's wiki follows this pattern with: - YAML frontmatter (type, slug, confidence, sensitive, created, updated) - Double-bracket slug wikilink syntax rendered by js/wiki.js - wiki/index.md as the slug registry (replaces a full search index for primary path) - Three-layer architecture: wiki primary → pgvector secondary → Claude fallback ## Why Karpathy, not standard RAG See [[ADR-007]] for full rationale. Short version: AccentOS knowledge is stable, domain-specific, human-curated, and small enough that markdown fetch beats vector embedding for primary path. The wiki pattern keeps the knowledge auditable and editable by non-engineers. ## Related [[karpathy-llm-wiki]] · [[ADR-007]]

# ADR-001: Supabase as primary database **Status**: Locked **Date**: 2026-04 (early AccentOS build) ## Decision Use Supabase (PostgreSQL + Row-Level Security + Auth) as the sole database and authentication layer for AccentOS. ## Context AccentOS needs a persistent backend for: vendor scores, customer CRM, quotes, pipeline, purchase orders, and all CRUD modules. Needs auth with 5 roles (Owner, Admin, Manager, Sales, Warehouse). ## Rationale - PostgreSQL with RLS lets each row be secured per-role without application-layer auth logic - Supabase REST API (PostgREST) = zero custom backend code; direct fetch() calls from JS - Free tier covers AccentOS scale (< 10 users, < 500MB) - Built-in auth with JWT; anon key embedded for public Cloudflare Pages deploy - Supabase MCP server available for Claude Code (AI can run SQL directly) ## Consequences - All schema changes require SQL migration files run by Michael (M## tasks) - No real-time subscriptions needed (pull-on-navigate pattern) - JWT auth means offline mode is limited to localStorage-backed features - pgvector extension available for M42/M43 RAG phase ## Alternatives considered - Firebase: rejected (NoSQL, weaker relational queries) - PlanetScale: rejected (no RLS, MySQL) - Self-hosted PostgreSQL: rejected (operational burden)

# ADR-005: 14-category vendor scoring rubric **Status**: Locked **Date**: 2026-05 (v6.9.4 scoring engine) ## Decision Score all vendors on 14 weighted categories (0–10 scale each). Weights sum to 100%. Tier assignment via adaptive percentile distribution over live scores. ## Context Accent needed an objective, defensible framework for comparing 240+ vendors across financial terms, marketing support, and market position. Subjective "gut feel" rankings were inconsistent and uncommunicable. ## Categories and weights Financial Terms: Rebates(10), Discounts(8), Credit Terms(6), Freight(8), Returns/RGA(7), IMAP/Markup(8) Sales/Marketing: Marketing Funds(7), Display(6), Lights America(5), Web Listing(4), Rep Score(8), DTC(7), L1 Member(6), Demand(10) ## Rationale - 14 categories cover every commercial dimension that affects Accent's economics - Numeric rubric (RUBRIC_NUMERIC) for objective categories: rebates%, discount%, credit days, freight threshold, IMAP multiplier, search volume - Composite scoring (RUBRIC_COMPONENTS) for multi-factor categories: marketing funds, display program - Binary scoring for L1 member, Lights America (binary gates, not spectrums) - Adaptive tier percentiles: tiers recalculate as new vendors are scored, avoiding fixed boundaries that age poorly - Weight scenarios support: current weights are default; Michael can test alternative weightings in the UI ## Consequences - 226 lower-volume vendors bulk-scored at 3 (deferred — see v6.9.3) - Top 30 by sales scored individually via

that age poorly - Weight scenarios support: current weights are default; Michael can test alternative weightings in the UI ## Consequences - 226 lower-volume vendors bulk-scored at 3 (deferred — see v6.9.3) - Top 30 by sales scored individually via web research - Score states: `scored | unverified | needs_review | n/a` tracked per category per vendor - Rubric pages: [[rubric-rebates]] through [[rubric-demand]] document the full scale ## Alternatives considered - Simple 5-point scale: rejected (loses nuance for numeric categories) - 20+ categories: rejected (diminishing returns, calibration burden) - Single financial score + qualitative notes: rejected (not sortable/rankable)

# ADR-007: Wiki-first RAG (Karpathy pattern) **Status**: Locked **Date**: 2026-05-06 (v6.11.1) ## Decision Implement knowledge retrieval using Andrej Karpathy's "LLM Wiki" pattern: curated markdown pages in `wiki/` as the primary grounding layer. pgvector/BM25 live-RAG is an optional secondary path. BUILD-RAG scripts are dev tools only. ## Context AccentOS's Ask the Engine had no grounding — it was a blank Claude API call. Adding domain knowledge grounding would dramatically improve answer quality for vendor scoring, lighting spec, and SOP queries. Two options: (1) maintain a curated wiki, or (2) build a live RAG pipeline with pgvector. ## Rationale - **Zero infra cost**: wiki/ = static files on Cloudflare Pages; no Supabase schema changes needed - **Zero latency overhead**: JS `fetch(wiki/concepts/slug.md)` is ~50ms vs ~200ms for embed + pgvector query - **Human-readable**: Michael can read, edit, and audit every knowledge page directly - **Explainable**: "Grounded on [rubric-rebates]" citation links to a readable page, not an opaque chunk - **Offline-safe**: wiki pages are cached by browser; pgvector needs live Supabase - **Confidence tracking**: frontmatter `confidence: low` flags pages needing review; no equivalent in pgvector chunks - pgvector path (M42/M43) remains available as secondary path when wiki has no hit ## Three-layer architecture ```

cached by browser; pgvector needs live Supabase - **Confidence tracking**: frontmatter `confidence: low` flags pages needing review; no equivalent in pgvector chunks - pgvector path (M42/M43) remains available as secondary path when wiki has no hit ## Three-layer architecture ``` wiki/ primary → pgvector secondary (optional) → pure Claude fallback ``` ## Consequences - All knowledge must be manually curated into wiki pages (no auto-ingest) - wiki_seed.py auto-generates module + vendor pages from source files (reduces manual burden) - wiki/log.md + wiki/hot.md provide session continuity without reading all source files - sendChat() modified: term overlap → fetch wiki → inject → fallback to pgvector - BUILD-RAG scripts: rag_build_index.py gives wiki chunks 1.3× BM25 boost ## Alternatives considered - Pure pgvector RAG: rejected (infra cost, M42/M43 required, opaque chunks) - No grounding: rejected (hallucination risk for vendor-specific questions) - External knowledge base (Notion, Confluence): rejected (another tool, another cost, no code integration)

# ADR-002: Vanilla JS, no framework **Status**: Locked **Date**: 2026-04 (early AccentOS build) ## Decision AccentOS is built in vanilla JavaScript with no build step, no bundler, no framework (no React, Vue, Angular, Svelte). ## Context AccentOS is a single-developer (Claude Code + Michael) project targeting a small internal team. Must deploy to Cloudflare Pages as static files without a build pipeline. ## Rationale - Zero build step: `git push` deploys immediately via Cloudflare Pages - No dependency management: no npm audit, no version conflicts, no node_modules - All module files are plain `<script src="js/module.js">` — trivially auditable - Vanilla DOM manipulation is sufficient for AccentOS's UI complexity - Claude Code can write, edit, and reason about vanilla JS without framework-specific knowledge - BUILD_INTELLIGENCE lesson: compact-CRUD modules ship in 5–8 min per module with vanilla JS pattern ## Consequences - No component reuse abstractions (each module is self-contained ~200–400 LOC) - Cross-module communication via global variables (CUSTOMERS, DEALS, etc.) - No TypeScript — type safety via code conventions and review - File size managed by extraction to js/*.js (ADR-004) - MODULE_REGISTRY refactor is a future polish item (reduce 4 shell touchpoints to 1) ## Alternatives considered - React: rejected (build

No TypeScript — type safety via code conventions and review - File size managed by extraction to js/*.js (ADR-004) - MODULE_REGISTRY refactor is a future polish item (reduce 4 shell touchpoints to 1) ## Alternatives considered - React: rejected (build step, overkill for CRUD modules) - Svelte: rejected (build step, less Claude Code familiarity) - Web Components: considered; deferred (adds complexity without clear payoff)

# ADR-003: Cloudflare Pages for hosting **Status**: Locked **Date**: 2026-04 (early AccentOS build) ## Decision Deploy AccentOS frontend to Cloudflare Pages. Deploy any server-side functions as Cloudflare Workers. ## Context AccentOS is a static SPA (single-page app). Auth is handled by Supabase. No server-side rendering needed. Must be accessible from mobile (showroom floor) and desktop. ## Rationale - Free tier: unlimited bandwidth, 500 builds/month, global CDN - Zero-config deployment: connect GitHub repo → push = deploy - Cloudflare Workers available for edge functions (e.g., Windward proxy, wiki search shim) - Global CDN: < 50ms TTFB from Wichita, KS (target user base) - Custom domain support on free tier ## Consequences - All sensitive keys (Supabase URL, Supabase API key, Anthropic API key) entered by user at runtime via Settings — never committed to repo - No server-side rendering, no SSR-dependent features - Workers needed for: API key proxying (if needed), Windward S5WebAPI integration (Track 6.11) - Build pipeline is: git push → Cloudflare Pages → deploy (< 60 seconds total) ## Alternatives considered - Vercel: considered; Cloudflare has better edge network and free Workers tier - GitHub Pages: rejected (no edge functions, no Workers) - AWS S3 + CloudFront: rejected

# ADR-004: File split strategy (index.html + js/*.js) **Status**: Locked **Date**: 2026-05 (v6.10.12 file split) ## Decision Keep index.html as the shell (auth, layout, shared utilities, inline JS for early-loaded modules). Extract larger/later-loaded modules to `js/<module>.js` files loaded via `<script src>` after the inline block. ## Context AccentOS grew to 800KB+ as a single index.html. Claude Code edit operations on large files are slow and error-prone. The 900KB trigger for file splits was established. ## Rationale - Each `<script src>` file is loaded globally — no import/export needed (ADR-002) - Module functions (e.g., `marketing(el)`) are globally accessible, so the goTo() dispatcher works transparently - Extraction done via awk/sed (for large blocks), Edit tool (for small changes) - BUILD_INTELLIGENCE lesson: `Edit ≤30 lines, awk/sed ≥100 lines` - True lazy-load deferred: adds 50+ LOC infrastructure + race condition risk with cross-module globals ## Split pattern ``` index.html ← shell: auth, CSS, layout, goTo, PAGE_META, inline JS for early modules js/module.js ← extracted at >900KB trigger; loaded via <script src> ``` New modules: write to `js/<name>.js`, add `<script src>` at bottom of index.html, add 4 shell touchpoints (sidebar entry, PAGE_META, pages dispatcher, hydrate call). ## Consequences - `let CUSTOMERS = []` in a

at >900KB trigger; loaded via <script src> ``` New modules: write to `js/<name>.js`, add `<script src>` at bottom of index.html, add 4 shell touchpoints (sidebar entry, PAGE_META, pages dispatcher, hydrate call). ## Consequences - `let CUSTOMERS = []` in a module file still attaches to window — cross-module reads work transparently - File size cap: ~900KB per file before triggering next split - Ordering matters: modules that depend on shared utils must load after those utils ## Alternatives considered - ES modules with import/export: rejected (would require rewriting all cross-module references) - Lazy-load on tab activation: deferred (race condition risk, 50+ LOC overhead)

# ADR-006: Customer Mode isolation in Ask the Engine **Status**: Locked **Date**: 2026-05 (v6.10.23) ## Decision Ask the Engine has two modes — Internal and Customer — with strict isolation. Customer mode never sees internal data (vendor names, margins, scores, costs, tier classifications, pricing strategy) or any wiki page with `sensitive: true`. ## Context AccentOS salespeople sometimes use a tablet in the showroom to answer customer questions. The same Knowledge Engine that powers internal analysis should not expose Accent's pricing margins or vendor relationships to customers. ## Internal mode - System: "Accent Lighting Intelligence Engine" — expert in vendor comparisons, margins, specs, quoting - Can reference vendor names, margin data, internal scores - Wiki sources: all pages except `sensitive: true` - Used by: Owner, Admin, Manager, Sales (backend) ## Customer mode - System: "Accent Lighting Consultant" — warm, consumer-facing assistant - NEVER reveals: being an AI on Claude, internal data, vendor names, margins - When specific inventory unknown: route to showroom visit or personalized quote - Wiki sources: concepts/ pages, lighting-reference cluster ONLY; skips entities/customers/, entities/vendors/ sensitive pages, all pages with `sensitive: true` - Used by: showroom floor, future public embed ## Rationale - Single chat component with mode toggle

showroom visit or personalized quote - Wiki sources: concepts/ pages, lighting-reference cluster ONLY; skips entities/customers/, entities/vendors/ sensitive pages, all pages with `sensitive: true` - Used by: showroom floor, future public embed ## Rationale - Single chat component with mode toggle keeps codebase lean - sessionStorage persistence: mode survives page navigation, resets on close (prevents accidental mode swap) - Customer mode prompt explicitly forbids AI disclosure — legal/brand protection - Wiki grounding in customer mode: safe to include lighting reference facts, not safe to include vendor deals or margins ## Consequences - sendChat() checks chatMode before injecting wiki context - `sensitive: true` frontmatter field in wiki pages enforces the boundary in code - Future: customer mode could be embedded as an iframe on accentlightinginc.com (post M18 site approval) ## Alternatives considered - Separate apps for internal vs customer: rejected (maintenance burden) - No customer mode: rejected (showroom use case is real and recurring)
